text:
I'd like to share some thoughts with you as you take this step.

Going back to school as an adult is one of the most courageous things you can do. You're not doing this because you have to, but because you want to. That distinction matters enormously. Every time you sit down with a textbook or attend a class, you're choosing to challenge yourself, and that's a position of real strength.

You'll probably notice things that younger students might miss. You have life experience behind you. You understand why you're there in a way that someone straight out of high school might not yet. You know what you want to learn and why it matters to you. That clarity is a superpower.

The hard parts are real, though. Balancing your studies with whatever else is happening in your life, whether that's work or family or just the accumulated responsibilities of being an adult, takes genuine effort. There will be moments when you're tired, when it feels like too much, when you wonder if you're crazy for doing this. That's normal. Almost everyone in your position feels that way sometimes. It doesn't mean you're making a mistake.

Be patient with yourself when you're not keeping up with the pace you imagined, or when a subject is harder than expected. You're not competing with anyone else. You're investing in yourself, and that's a long game. Progress is progress, even if it's slower than you hoped.

The people around you matter too. Lean on them. If you have friends, family, or classmates who understand what you're doing, tell them when you need help or encouragement. And remember that you're not the first person to do this. You're part of a whole community of people who decided that learning was worth the sacrifice.

When you finish, you'll have done something remarkable. But you don't need to wait until then to feel proud. That feeling can start now, with every class you show up for, every assignment you complete, every concept you finally understand. You're already doing it.
